← World Gallery
Regions Artists All works

All Works

Every piece, searchable. Type a title, culture, artist, period, or region.
371 works
Africa
Bronze Head from Ife
Yoruba · 12th–15th c. · Nigeria
Naturalistic portraits cast in brass with lost-wax technique. When first seen in Europe they were disbelieved as African work.
Africa
Benin Bronzes
Edo kingdom · 16th–17th c. · Nigeria
Plaques that once covered pillars of the royal palace in Benin City. Looted in 1897; still being repatriated today.
Africa
Bronze Head of Queen Idia
Benin kingdom · 16th c. · Nigeria
Portrait of the first Queen Mother of Benin, cast to sit on an ancestral altar. One of the most famous of the Benin bronzes.
Africa
Igbo-Ukwu Bronze
Igbo · 9th c. · Nigeria
Lost-wax castings of startling delicacy — three centuries before Ife, and predating most European bronzework of the same technical sophistication.
Africa
Great Zimbabwe
Shona · 11th–15th c. · Zimbabwe
Dry-stone walls built without mortar, enclosing a trade capital that controlled the gold road to the Indian Ocean.
Africa
Great Mosque of Kilwa
Swahili coast · 11th–14th c. · Tanzania
Coral-stone mosque on an island that dominated Indian Ocean trade. Ibn Battuta called Kilwa "one of the most beautiful cities in the world."
Africa
Rock-Hewn Churches of Lalibela
Zagwe dynasty · 12th–13th c. · Ethiopia
Eleven churches carved downward into solid rock. Meant as a new Jerusalem after pilgrimage routes were cut off.
Africa
Great Mosque of Djenné
Mali · rebuilt 1907 (earlier mosque 13th c.)
The world's largest mud-brick building. Every spring the whole town replasters it by hand in a single day.
Africa
Dogon Cliff Villages
Dogon · 14th c. onward · Mali
Mud granaries and shrines stacked along the Bandiagara Escarpment. The Dogon moved here fleeing forced conversion and stayed half a millennium.
Africa
Kente Cloth
Asante & Ewe · 17th c. onward · Ghana
Hand-woven silk and cotton strips sewn together into royal cloth. Each pattern has a name and a proverb behind it.
Africa
Obelisks of Aksum
Aksumite kingdom · 4th c. · Ethiopia
Granite stelae marking royal graves; the tallest once stood nearly a hundred feet high. One was looted by Mussolini, returned in 2005.
Africa
Nkisi Power Figure
Kongo · 19th–20th c. · Central Africa
Wooden figures bristling with nails and blades. Each nail driven in activated a pledge, a curse, or a contract witnessed by the spirit inside.
Africa
Senufo Sculpture
Senufo · West Africa
Elongated female figures and carved divination stools used by the Poro and Sandogo societies in Côte d'Ivoire and Mali.
Africa
Fang Reliquary (Byeri)
Fang · 19th–20th c. · Gabon / Cameroon
Carved guardian heads placed atop boxes of ancestral bones. Picasso saw one in 1907 and the Demoiselles d'Avignon followed.
Africa
Akan Goldweights
Asante / Akan · 15th–19th c. · Ghana
Brass figurines used to weigh gold dust. Each one is also a proverb — the weight you balance against is a riddle.
Africa
Lydenburg Heads
Early Iron Age · 500 CE · South Africa
Terracotta heads found buried under a farm in Mpumalanga — the earliest known African figurative sculpture south of the equator.
Africa
San Rock Art
San · up to 30,000 years old · Southern Africa
Eland, hunters, and trance figures painted on rock shelters from Namibia to the Drakensberg. One of humanity's longest unbroken painting traditions.
Africa
Golden Stool of the Asante
Asante · 18th c. · Ghana
Sacred seat that is said to contain the soul of the Asante nation. No one — not even the king — sits on it. A British demand to do so in 1900 sparked war.
Africa
Ibeji Twin Figures
Yoruba · 19th c. onward · Nigeria
When a twin dies, a carved figure stands in — fed, dressed, and cared for like the living. The Yoruba have one of the highest twinning rates in the world.
Africa
Larabanga Mosque
Sudano-Sahelian · 15th c. · Ghana
A mud-and-stick mosque painted white, bristling with wooden supports. The oldest mosque in West Africa still in its original form.
Africa
Queen Mother Pendant Mask (Iyoba)
Edo kingdom · 16th c. · Nigeria
Ivory mask worn on the belt of the Oba of Benin. The forehead tiaras are miniature Portuguese heads — trading partners carved as ornament.
Africa
Nok Terracotta
Nok culture · 500 BCE – 500 CE · Nigeria
Hollow terracotta heads with triangular eyes and pierced pupils. The oldest known figurative sculpture in sub-Saharan Africa.
Africa
Mapungubwe Hill
Mapungubwe Kingdom · 1075–1220 · South Africa
A sandstone hill-top capital that controlled Indian Ocean gold and ivory trade centuries before Great Zimbabwe. A golden rhinoceros was dug up from a royal grave here.
Africa
Ndop — Kuba King Portrait
Kuba Kingdom · 17th c. onward · DR Congo
Wooden royal portraits showing each Kuba king with a personal emblem — a drum, an anvil, a game board. Stand-ins who keep the king present after death.
Africa
Baule Sculpture
Baule · 17th c. onward · Côte d'Ivoire
Delicate wooden figures of "spirit spouses" from a parallel world, and calm portrait masks carved for Goli festivals. Among the most influential African sculptural traditions.
Africa
Asante Jewelry
Akan / Asante · 19th c. · Ghana
Gold jewelry for the chief and his court — beads, pectorals, rings cast with the same lost-wax technique as the goldweights. Sumptuary law regulated who could wear what.
Africa
Ethiopian Processional Cross
Ethiopian Orthodox · 12th c. onward · Ethiopia
Hand-held metal crosses carried at the front of church processions. Each is unique — geometries nested inside geometries, hammered out by a parish smith.
Africa
Brandberg Rock Paintings
San · up to 2,000 years old · Namibia
The Brandberg Massif hides thousands of rock paintings, most famously the "White Lady" — not a lady at all, but a medicine figure. Painted for ritual, not ornament.
Africa
Twyfelfontein Petroglyphs
Pre-San hunter-gatherers · 6,000 years old · Namibia
Thousands of rock engravings of giraffes, rhinos, and lions on sandstone. A seal, 900 km from the nearest sea, suggests long-distance travel — or long memory.
Egypt & Nubia
Great Pyramid of Giza
Old Kingdom · c. 2560 BCE
Tomb of Khufu. 2.3 million stone blocks, averaging 2.5 tons each. The tallest human-made structure on earth for 3,800 years.
Egypt & Nubia
Great Sphinx of Giza
Old Kingdom · c. 2500 BCE
Carved from a single limestone outcrop, the oldest known monumental sculpture. Its nose was already missing before Napoleon arrived.
Egypt & Nubia
Narmer Palette
Early Dynastic · c. 3100 BCE
A ceremonial siltstone palette showing a king smiting an enemy — one of the earliest monuments of unified Egypt, with its conventions already fully formed.
Egypt & Nubia
Bust of Nefertiti
Amarna period · c. 1345 BCE
Painted limestone, found in the workshop of the sculptor Thutmose at Amarna. Still in Berlin; Egypt has asked for it back for a century.
Egypt & Nubia
Mask of Tutankhamun
New Kingdom · c. 1323 BCE
Eleven kilograms of solid gold, inlaid with lapis, carnelian, and obsidian. The funerary mask of a boy-king who died at nineteen.
Egypt & Nubia
Karnak Temple Complex
New Kingdom onward · 2000 BCE – 100 CE
A city of temples at Thebes, built and rebuilt by thirty dynasties. The hypostyle hall alone has 134 columns.
Egypt & Nubia
Temple of Hatshepsut
New Kingdom · c. 1470 BCE · Deir el-Bahari
Three stacked colonnaded terraces built into a cliff for Egypt's longest-reigning female pharaoh. Her successor tried to erase her — and failed.
Egypt & Nubia
Tomb of Nefertari
19th dynasty · c. 1255 BCE · Valley of the Queens
Painted for Ramesses II's favorite wife — the queen walks through the underworld, plays the board game Senet, and greets the gods. Colors still vivid.
Egypt & Nubia
Abu Simbel
Reign of Ramesses II · c. 1264 BCE
Four colossal statues guard the temple front. In the 1960s the whole complex was cut apart and moved 200 feet up to escape Lake Nasser.
Egypt & Nubia
Rosetta Stone
Ptolemaic · 196 BCE
A decree written three times — in hieroglyphs, Demotic, and Greek — that became the key to reading Egyptian. Champollion cracked it in 1822.
Egypt & Nubia
Pyramids of Meroë
Kingdom of Kush · 300 BCE – 300 CE · Sudan
Over 200 pyramids, steeper and smaller than Egypt's, built for Nubian royalty. Sudan has more pyramids than Egypt — about twice as many.
Egypt & Nubia
Book of the Dead
New Kingdom onward · 1550 BCE – 50 BCE
Spells written on papyrus to guide the deceased through the Duat. The Weighing of the Heart against a feather is its most famous scene.
Egypt & Nubia
Colossi of Memnon
New Kingdom · c. 1350 BCE · Luxor
Twin seated statues of Amenhotep III, sixty feet tall. After an earthquake cracked one, it "sang" at dawn for two centuries — until Romans repaired it.
Egypt & Nubia
Fayum Mummy Portraits
Roman Egypt · 1st–3rd c. CE
Encaustic panels placed over the faces of mummies. The most personal, most realistic portraits to survive from the ancient world — they still look you in the eye.
Egypt & Nubia
Temple of Dendur
Roman Egypt · 15 BCE
A small sandstone temple given to the United States in 1965 when Lake Nasser was about to drown it. Reassembled stone by stone inside the Met.
Egypt & Nubia
Pyramid of Djoser
Old Kingdom · c. 2670 BCE · Saqqara
The first pyramid in Egypt — six stacked mastabas designed by the architect Imhotep, later deified as a god of wisdom and medicine.
Egypt & Nubia
Luxor Temple
New Kingdom · Amenhotep III / Ramesses II · c. 1400–1250 BCE
Every year during the Opet Festival, statues of the gods were carried in boats from Karnak to here. A Roman camp, Christian church, and mosque all followed.
Egypt & Nubia
Temple of Edfu
Ptolemaic · 237–57 BCE
The best-preserved temple in Egypt, because it was buried in desert sand for centuries. Dedicated to Horus, with reliefs of his battle against Seth.
Egypt & Nubia
Tomb of Seti I
19th Dynasty · c. 1279 BCE · Valley of the Kings
The longest, deepest, and most richly painted tomb in the valley. Every wall is covered in funerary texts; ceilings map the stars.
Egypt & Nubia
Colossal Statue of Akhenaten
Amarna period · c. 1350 BCE
The heretic pharaoh depicted with a long face, wide hips, and swollen belly — a deliberate break from centuries of idealized royal portraiture.
Egypt & Nubia
Ramesseum
New Kingdom · Ramesses II · 13th c. BCE · Thebes
Memorial temple of Ramesses II. A collapsed colossus here inspired Shelley's "Ozymandias" — though Shelley never saw it.
Egypt & Nubia
Medinet Habu
New Kingdom · Ramesses III · c. 1150 BCE · Thebes
The fortress-temple of the last great warrior pharaoh. Its walls narrate the repulse of the Sea Peoples — history preserved because the victor carved it.
Egypt & Nubia
Dendera Temple
Ptolemaic → Roman · 54 BCE – 20 CE
Temple of Hathor. Its astronomical ceilings include the only known circular zodiac from ancient Egypt — now a replica; the original is in the Louvre.
Egypt & Nubia
Kom Ombo
Ptolemaic · 2nd c. BCE
A rare double temple — one half for the crocodile god Sobek, the other for Horus the Elder. Mummified crocodiles still fill a side chamber.
Egypt & Nubia
Tomb of Tutankhamun
18th Dynasty · c. 1323 BCE · Valley of the Kings
A small, hastily decorated tomb, overlooked by thieves for three thousand years. Howard Carter opened it in 1922 and the world went Egyptomaniac again.
Egypt & Nubia
The Seated Scribe
Old Kingdom · c. 2620–2500 BCE · Saqqara
Painted limestone, eyes inlaid with crystal and copper. Alert and ready, reed in hand — the realist portrait of a bureaucrat, 4,500 years ago.
Egypt & Nubia
Khafre Enthroned
Old Kingdom · c. 2570 BCE · Giza
Diorite portrait of the pharaoh who built the second pyramid. Horus the falcon wraps his wings around the back of the king's head — divine kingship locked in stone.
Egypt & Nubia
Pyramid of Meidum
Old Kingdom · c. 2600 BCE
A failed smooth-sided pyramid whose outer casing collapsed. What survives is the blunt tower at the core — an architectural experiment that taught the builders of Giza.
Egypt & Nubia
Sphinx of Taharqa
Nubian Kush · 25th Dynasty · c. 680 BCE
A Nubian pharaoh in the form of an Egyptian sphinx, carrying a double crown he had earned as conqueror. Now in the British Museum.
Egypt & Nubia
Great Sphinx of Tanis
Old to Middle Kingdom · c. 2600–1800 BCE
One of the largest sphinxes outside Giza — reused and re-inscribed by pharaoh after pharaoh. Now guards the entrance of the Louvre's Egyptian galleries.
Egypt & Nubia
Mastaba of Ti
Old Kingdom · c. 2400 BCE · Saqqara
The tomb of a minor royal official, painted inside with daily-life scenes — farming, fishing, ships under sail — and regarded as the high point of Old Kingdom relief.
Egypt & Nubia
Dendera Zodiac
Ptolemaic · c. 50 BCE
A circular sandstone relief of the night sky carried off from the ceiling of Dendera temple by a French explorer in 1821. Now in the Louvre; a replica hangs in the original.
Egypt & Nubia
Prisse Papyrus
Middle Kingdom · c. 1900 BCE
The oldest literary book in existence. Two teachings of wisdom — "the Instructions of Kagemni" and "of Ptahhotep" — carefully written in cursive hieroglyphs.
Egypt & Nubia
Reliefs from the Tomb of Seti I
c. 1290 BCE · Painted limestone · Sully Wing
The goddess Hathor welcomes Seti I into the afterlife. Cut from the walls of KV17 in the Valley of the Kings in the 19th century — a practice we now consider vandalism, but which saved this relief from the flooding that ravaged the tomb.
Egypt & Nubia
Stele of King Djet (The Serpent King)
c. 3000 BCE · Limestone · Sully Wing
Over 5,000 years old — from the very dawn of writing. A serpent (the king's name) stands above the serekh, the palace façade, topped by Horus the falcon. This is what Egyptian writing looked like before it became hieroglyphs.
Mesopotamia & Persia
Warka Vase
Uruk period · c. 3200 BCE · Sumer
One of the earliest known narrative reliefs — a procession to the goddess Inanna. Looted from the Iraq Museum in 2003 and later recovered, broken.
Mesopotamia & Persia
Standard of Ur
Sumerian · c. 2600 BCE
A wooden box inlaid with lapis, shell, and red limestone. One side shows war; the other, a banquet after the victory.
Mesopotamia & Persia
Ziggurat of Ur
Neo-Sumerian · c. 2100 BCE · Iraq
A stepped temple-platform to the moon god Nanna. Its baked-brick facade has been partly restored; the core is as old as the pyramids.
Mesopotamia & Persia
Code of Hammurabi
Old Babylonian · c. 1754 BCE
Diorite stele, seven feet tall. 282 laws in cuneiform — "an eye for an eye" comes from here. Now in the Louvre.
Mesopotamia & Persia
Lamassu
Neo-Assyrian · 9th–7th c. BCE
Winged bulls with human heads, guarding palace gates at Nineveh and Nimrud. Carved with five legs so they look correct from front and side.
Mesopotamia & Persia
Lion Hunt of Ashurbanipal
Neo-Assyrian · c. 645 BCE · Nineveh
Alabaster reliefs from the king's palace. A dying lioness drags paralyzed hind legs — one of the most unnervingly precise animal studies in ancient art.
Mesopotamia & Persia
Ishtar Gate
Neo-Babylonian · c. 575 BCE
Glazed brick gate to Babylon under Nebuchadnezzar II. Dragons and bulls in cobalt blue. Reconstructed in Berlin's Pergamon Museum.
Mesopotamia & Persia
Cyrus Cylinder
Achaemenid Persia · c. 539 BCE
A clay cylinder inscribed after Cyrus took Babylon. Often called the first charter of human rights — a claim the British Museum treats with more caution.
Mesopotamia & Persia
Persepolis
Achaemenid Persia · 518 BCE onward · Iran
Ceremonial capital of Darius and Xerxes. Reliefs show envoys from every corner of the empire bringing tribute. Burned by Alexander in 330 BCE.
Mesopotamia & Persia
Naqsh-e Rustam
Achaemenid & Sasanian · 5th c. BCE – 3rd c. CE · Iran
Cruciform tombs cut high into a cliff for Darius the Great and his heirs. Centuries later the Sasanians carved their own triumphs below.
Mesopotamia & Persia
Mask of Warka
Uruk · c. 3100 BCE · Sumer
The first known naturalistic human face in art — a white marble mask, probably of the goddess Inanna. Looted in 2003, recovered in a farmer's garden.
Mesopotamia & Persia
Chogha Zanbil
Elamite · c. 1250 BCE · Iran
The best-preserved ziggurat in the world — five stepped stages of mud brick, built for the god Inshushinak and abandoned when the city fell.
Mesopotamia & Persia
Behistun Inscription
Achaemenid Persia · 520 BCE · Iran
Darius the Great's autobiography carved three hundred feet up a cliff, in three languages. The trilingual key that let Rawlinson decipher cuneiform.
Mesopotamia & Persia
Tomb of Cyrus at Pasargadae
Achaemenid Persia · c. 530 BCE · Iran
A simple stepped stone tomb for the founder of the empire. Alexander, visiting 200 years later, found it looted and ordered it restored.
Mesopotamia & Persia
Oxus Treasure
Achaemenid Persia · 5th–4th c. BCE · Tajikistan
180 gold and silver objects washed out of a riverbed — chariots, lions, votive plaques. The British Museum's greatest hoard of Achaemenid metalwork.
Mesopotamia & Persia
Naqsh-e Rajab
Sasanian · 3rd c. CE · Iran
Rock reliefs near Persepolis celebrating the founder of the Sasanian empire. Ardashir receives the crown directly from Ahura Mazda — politics cast as theology.
Mesopotamia & Persia
Taq Kasra
Sasanian · 3rd–6th c. CE · Iraq
The largest single-span brick vault in the ancient world — the throne hall of the Sasanian palace at Ctesiphon. It still stands, barely.
Mesopotamia & Persia
Ram in a Thicket
Sumerian · c. 2500 BCE · Royal Cemetery of Ur
A goat rearing against a golden bush, made of wood, gold leaf, silver, lapis, and shell. Leonard Woolley named it for Abraham's offering in Genesis.
Mesopotamia & Persia
Apadana Reliefs
Achaemenid Persia · c. 500 BCE · Persepolis
The great staircase of the audience hall. Subject peoples from 23 nations process forward with tribute — Ethiopians with ivory, Lydians with bowls, Bactrian camels.
Mesopotamia & Persia
Nimrud
Neo-Assyrian · 879 BCE onward · Iraq
Ashurnasirpal II's Northwest Palace, once lined with alabaster reliefs of hunt and war. ISIS dynamited the site in 2015; much had already been moved to museums.
Mesopotamia & Persia
Babylon
Old Babylonian → Neo-Babylonian · 18th c. BCE – 6th c. BCE
Capital of Hammurabi, Nebuchadnezzar, and Cyrus. Reconstructed in the 1980s by Saddam Hussein with bricks stamped in his own name.
Mesopotamia & Persia
Mesopotamian Cylinder Seals
Sumerian onward · 4th millennium BCE onward
Tiny carved stone cylinders rolled across clay to sign a document. Each person owned one — an identity card and an art form at once.
Mesopotamia & Persia
Statue of Ebih-Il
Sumerian · c. 2400 BCE · Mari
An administrator from Mari, seated with hands folded in prayer, dressed in a tufted skirt. Lapis and shell eyes still stare from a white gypsum face.
Mesopotamia & Persia
Victory Stele of Naram-Sin
Akkadian · c. 2250 BCE
The king climbs a mountain over dying enemies, horned helmet marking him as divine. The first ruler in history to have himself depicted as a god.
Mesopotamia & Persia
Burney Relief
Old Babylonian · c. 1800 BCE
A nude winged goddess standing on two lions, flanked by owls. Most likely Ereshkigal, queen of the underworld — or Inanna, descending into it.
Mesopotamia & Persia
Black Obelisk of Shalmaneser III
Neo-Assyrian · c. 825 BCE
A tribute list in stone — foreign kings bringing gifts to Shalmaneser. One is Jehu of Israel, bowing to kiss the ground. The earliest known image of an Israelite.
Mesopotamia & Persia
Hatra
Parthian · 1st–3rd c. CE · Iraq
A desert caravan city whose kings commissioned limestone statues fusing Hellenistic drapery with Parthian dress. Destroyed by ISIS in 2015.
Mesopotamia & Persia
Stele of the Vultures
Sumerian · c. 2450 BCE · Lagash
The earliest known battle monument. King Eannatum's army marches in lockstep behind their shields; on the other side, vultures carry off the heads of the defeated.
Mesopotamia & Persia
Queen Puabi's Headdress
Sumerian · c. 2600 BCE · Ur
Gold leaves, lapis lazuli pendants, carnelian beads, tied with ribbon hair-wire. Queen Puabi went to her grave wearing this, attended by 23 servants — also buried.
Mesopotamia & Persia
Royal Cemetery of Ur
Sumerian · c. 2600 BCE · Iraq
Sixteen royal graves, each with servants, soldiers, and musicians sacrificed and buried beside the king. Out came the Standard of Ur, Queen Puabi's headdress, and the Ram in the Thicket.
Mesopotamia & Persia
Yazılıkaya
Hittite · 13th c. BCE · Turkey
An open-air rock sanctuary near Hattusa. Sixty-six gods and goddesses walk in procession along the cliff face — the full Hittite pantheon gathered in stone.
Mesopotamia & Persia
Lamassu of Khorsabad
c. 713–706 BCE · Gypsum alabaster · Richelieu Wing
Human-headed winged bulls, each over 4 meters tall. They guarded the gates of Sargon II's palace in what is now northern Iraq. Carved with five legs so that they appear to be standing still when seen from the front, and striding forward when seen from the side.
Mesopotamia & Persia
Frieze of the Archers of Darius I
c. 510 BCE · Glazed brick · Richelieu Wing
From the palace of Darius the Great at Susa. A procession of the king's elite guard — the "Immortals" — in brilliantly colored glazed brick. Each carries a spear, bow, and quiver; each face is subtly different from the next.
Mediterranean
Palace of Knossos
Minoan · c. 1900 BCE · Crete
A sprawling Bronze Age palace with running water, frescoes of dolphins and bull-leapers, and a storage maze that may be the origin of the Minotaur myth.
Mediterranean
The Parthenon
Classical Greek · 447–432 BCE · Athens
Temple to Athena on the Acropolis. The columns lean inward and swell slightly in the middle — optical corrections so they look perfectly straight.
Mediterranean
Charioteer of Delphi
Early Classical · c. 478 BCE
One of the few surviving Greek bronzes. His calm expression, inset with glass and copper eyes, is a moment in a victory lap — all force held still.
Mediterranean
Discobolus
Classical Greek · c. 450 BCE (Roman copy)
Myron's discus thrower — the original bronze is lost; only Roman marble copies survive. A moment just before release, coiled and still.
Mediterranean
Venus de Milo
Hellenistic Greek · c. 130–100 BCE
Marble Aphrodite found on the island of Milos in 1820. The missing arms are part of her fame — we don't know what she was doing.
Mediterranean
Winged Victory of Samothrace
Hellenistic · c. 200 BCE
Nike landing on the prow of a ship, wind pressing her drapery against her body. One of the most physically dynamic pieces of ancient sculpture.
Mediterranean
Laocoön and His Sons
Hellenistic · 1st c. BCE – 1st c. CE
A Trojan priest and his sons strangled by sea serpents. Dug up in a vineyard in 1506 and hauled to the Vatican. Michelangelo studied it for weeks.
Mediterranean
Augustus of Prima Porta
Early Roman Empire · c. 15 CE
The emperor posed as a general, barefoot like a god. The breastplate shows the return of captured Roman standards — a diplomatic win dressed as conquest.
Mediterranean
The Colosseum
Flavian · 70–80 CE · Rome
An elliptical amphitheater for 50,000 spectators. The underground hypogeum ran elevators, flooded the floor for sea battles, and housed wild animals.
Mediterranean
Trajan's Column
Imperial Roman · 113 CE
A 100-foot marble column whose spiral frieze narrates the Dacian Wars in 155 continuous scenes — history carved as a comic strip.
Mediterranean
Pompeii
Roman · buried 79 CE
Vesuvius sealed a whole Roman town in volcanic ash. Frescoes, bread loaves, graffiti, and the shapes of bodies all survived — daily life frozen.
Mediterranean
Riace Bronzes
Classical Greek · c. 460–450 BCE · Italy
Two life-size bronze warriors pulled from the sea off Calabria in 1972. Their teeth are silver, their eyelashes hammered copper.
Mediterranean
Aphrodite of Knidos
Classical Greek · Praxiteles · c. 360 BCE
The first free-standing nude female figure in Greek sculpture — scandalous enough that men climbed her sanctuary walls at night. Only copies survive.
Mediterranean
Kroisos Kouros
Archaic Greek · c. 530 BCE
A young man stepping forward, hands at his sides, slight smile — the archaic formula. Found on a grave at Anavyssos, one leg at a time.
Mediterranean
Ara Pacis
Augustan Rome · 13–9 BCE
The Altar of Peace. The imperial family processes in carved marble, framed by acanthus tendrils. Augustus inserts himself into the mythic past.
Mediterranean
Pantheon
Roman Imperial · 126 CE · Rome
The largest unreinforced concrete dome ever built. The oculus at its center has been open to the sky for nineteen centuries — rain falls through it into drains in the floor.
Mediterranean
Apoxyomenos
Classical Greek · Lysippos · c. 330 BCE
An athlete scraping oil from his arm. The Roman marble copy stands in for a lost bronze original by Alexander the Great's favorite sculptor.
Mediterranean
Sarcophagus of the Spouses
Etruscan · c. 520 BCE · Italy
A painted terracotta couple reclining together on their tomb lid — smiling, touching, treated as equals. Unthinkable in contemporary Greek or Egyptian funerary art.
Mediterranean
Doryphoros
Classical Greek · Polykleitos · c. 440 BCE
The "Spear Bearer" — a treatise on ideal human proportion made in marble. Polykleitos wrote a book called the Canon about it; only this statue survives.
Mediterranean
Dying Gaul
Hellenistic · c. 230–220 BCE (Roman copy)
A defeated Celt collapsing on his shield, wound dripping. Pergamene art showed the enemy as human — a compliment paid in marble, after the kill.
Mediterranean
Boxer at Rest
Hellenistic · c. 100 BCE
A bronze boxer after a fight — cauliflower ears, bloodied lip, exhausted. One of the few surviving Greek originals, not a later copy.
Mediterranean
Boy with Thorn
Hellenistic → Roman · 1st c. BCE (later copies)
A boy pulling a thorn from his foot, head bent in total concentration. One of the first ancient bronzes to be widely copied in the Renaissance.
Mediterranean
Hadrian's Villa
Roman Imperial · 117–138 CE · Tivoli
A 250-acre retreat where the emperor built scale models of the sights from his tour of the empire — a Nile canal, an Athenian stoa, a Canopus.
Mediterranean
Kritios Boy
Early Classical Greek · c. 480 BCE
The hinge between Archaic and Classical — the first marble body where weight shifts onto one leg (contrapposto), giving the figure life.
Mediterranean
Artemision Bronze
Classical Greek · c. 460 BCE
Pulled from a Roman shipwreck off Cape Artemision. Either Zeus throwing a thunderbolt or Poseidon throwing a trident — his missing weapon makes it impossible to say.
Mediterranean
Erechtheion
Classical Greek · 421–406 BCE · Athens
Temple on the sacred north side of the Acropolis. Six marble maidens (the Caryatids) hold up the porch in place of columns.
Mediterranean
Alexander Mosaic
Roman copy of Hellenistic painting · c. 100 BCE · Pompeii
Alexander charging Darius at Issus, rendered in a million tiny tesserae. The lost original oil painting was probably by Philoxenos of Eretria.
Mediterranean
Lady of Elche
Iberian · c. 4th c. BCE · Spain
A limestone bust with enormous spoked headdress-wheels over the ears. Iberian, not Greek — the style belongs to a world that left no writing to explain itself.
Mediterranean
Mask of Agamemnon
Mycenaean · c. 1550 BCE
A beaten-gold funerary mask from Grave Circle A at Mycenae. Schliemann announced he had "looked upon the face of Agamemnon" — though this mask is 300 years too early.
Mediterranean
Capitoline Wolf
Etruscan or Medieval bronze · 5th c. BCE or 13th c. CE
The she-wolf nursing Romulus and Remus — emblem of Rome. Once thought ancient Etruscan; recent analysis suggests a medieval re-casting.
Mediterranean
Chimera of Arezzo
Etruscan · c. 400 BCE
Bronze of the mythical lion-goat-snake beast, wounded and snarling. Found in 1553 and immediately cleaned up by Cellini; the inscription on its leg is Etruscan, not Latin.
Mediterranean
Pergamon Altar
Hellenistic · c. 180–160 BCE · Pergamon
A colossal marble frieze of the gods fighting the giants — deep-cut writhing bodies that may have inspired Michelangelo. The whole structure was hauled to Berlin in 1880.
Mediterranean
Barberini Faun
Hellenistic · c. 220 BCE
A sleeping satyr sprawled drunk on a rock, legs splayed, utterly without dignity. Excavated from Hadrian's mausoleum moat — perhaps thrown over the walls during a siege.
Mediterranean
Portland Vase
Roman cameo glass · c. 1–25 CE
A cobalt-blue glass vase dipped in white and carved back through the outer layer. One of the finest surviving pieces of Roman cameo glass — and inspiration for Wedgwood's first portraits.
Mediterranean
Equestrian Statue of Marcus Aurelius
Roman Imperial · c. 175 CE
The only surviving large Roman bronze equestrian. It lived because Christians mistook it for Constantine. Now replaced by a copy in the Campidoglio; the original is inside.
Mediterranean
Parthenon Frieze
Classical Greek · Pheidias workshop · c. 440 BCE
A continuous band 160 meters long, showing the Panathenaic procession — horses, musicians, elders, gods receiving the city's tribute. Half survives in the British Museum, half in Athens.
Mediterranean
Herculaneum
Roman · buried 79 CE · Italy
Pompeii's smaller, richer neighbor. Volcanic mud sealed it instead of ash, so wooden beams, baby cradles, and carbonized scrolls all survived.
Mediterranean
Borghese Gladiator
c. 100 BCE · Marble · Sully Wing
Not actually a gladiator — a warrior fighting a mounted opponent, arm raised with a (lost) shield. A tour de force of anatomical study; every muscle is taut with the effort of the moment before impact.
Mediterranean
Sleeping Hermaphroditus
Roman copy of a Greek original · 2nd century CE · Sully Wing
From one angle, a sleeping woman. Walk around to the other side: the body is both. The mattress beneath — realistic enough that you want to touch it — was carved by Bernini in 1619 when the statue was restored.
Europe
Book of Kells
Insular Celtic · c. 800 CE · Ireland
A Latin gospel book from monks on Iona and Kells. The ornament is so intricate that Gerald of Wales thought it "the work of an angel, not of a man."
Europe
Chartres Cathedral
High Gothic · 1194–1220 · France
Rebuilt in 26 years after a fire. Its stained glass — especially the "Chartres blue" — has never been matched; the recipe was lost.
Europe
The Arnolfini Portrait
Early Netherlandish · Jan van Eyck · 1434
A merchant and his wife in a Bruges interior. The convex mirror on the back wall reflects two more figures — one of them possibly the painter.
Europe
The School of Athens
High Renaissance · Raphael · 1509–1511
Plato and Aristotle at the center, surrounded by the Greek philosophical tradition. Raphael slipped portraits of himself and Michelangelo into the crowd.
Europe
David
High Renaissance · Michelangelo · 1501–1504
Seventeen feet of marble carved from a block others had given up on. David faces Goliath before the stone flies — tense, not triumphant.
Europe
Sistine Chapel Ceiling
High Renaissance · Michelangelo · 1508–1512
Four years, mostly alone, on scaffolding. Michelangelo considered himself a sculptor, not a painter, and complained about the job the whole way through.
Europe
The Night Watch
Dutch Golden Age · Rembrandt · 1642
A militia portrait transformed into a theatrical scene — figures stepping forward, a drumbeat, a girl glowing in the crowd. Not actually set at night.
Europe
Las Meninas
Spanish Golden Age · Velázquez · 1656
The Infanta and her attendants — but who is the painting for? The king and queen appear only in the mirror. Foucault wrote an essay on that mirror.
Europe
Girl with a Pearl Earring
Dutch Golden Age · Vermeer · c. 1665
Not a portrait but a "tronie" — a study of an imagined figure. The pearl itself is two quick strokes of white paint. We don't know who she was.
Europe
The Starry Night
Post-Impressionism · Van Gogh · 1889
Painted from the window of his asylum room at Saint-Rémy, from memory. The village below is imagined; the swirling sky he said he could actually see.
Europe
The Scream
Expressionism · Edvard Munch · 1893
Munch wrote in his diary: "I sensed an infinite scream passing through nature." The sky's red may be from the Krakatoa eruption ten years earlier.
Europe
Bayeux Tapestry
Norman · c. 1070 · France
Seventy meters of embroidered linen telling the Norman conquest of England. Probably stitched by English nuns for the new conqueror's half-brother.
Europe
Ghent Altarpiece
Early Netherlandish · Jan van Eyck · 1432 · Belgium
Twelve folding panels of unmatched detail. Stolen seven times across its history; the Just Judges panel has been missing since 1934.
Europe
The Birth of Venus
Early Renaissance · Botticelli · c. 1485 · Florence
Venus rises from the sea on a shell, blown to shore by wind-gods. Painted on canvas when fresco was still the fashion — and kept hidden from Savonarola's fires.
Europe
The Last Supper
High Renaissance · Leonardo · 1495–1498 · Milan
Painted on a refectory wall in an experimental technique that began flaking within decades. The fragility is part of the legend.
Europe
The Garden of Earthly Delights
Early Netherlandish · Bosch · c. 1500 · Spain
A triptych of Eden, excess, and hell. The right panel is crowded with monsters nobody has fully decoded — including one with sheet music tattooed on a buttock.
Europe
Impression, Sunrise
Impressionism · Monet · 1872 · Le Havre
The painting that gave a movement its name — a hostile critic meant "impression" as an insult. Hazy harbor, red sun, no detail that isn't a brushstroke.
Europe
The Kiss
Vienna Secession · Klimt · 1907–1908
Gold leaf and oil on canvas. A couple fused inside a shimmering shroud of pattern — Klimt's answer to the new century.
Europe
Notre-Dame de Paris
Gothic · 1163–1345 · Paris
Cathedral of the kings of France. Half the fabric is original medieval, half is Viollet-le-Duc's 19th-century reimagining. The 2019 fire took the spire.
Europe
The Isenheim Altarpiece
Northern Renaissance · Grünewald · 1512–1516 · Alsace
Painted for a hospital for plague and ergotism. Christ's body on the cross is covered in exactly the sores the patients saw on their own.
Europe
Primavera
Early Renaissance · Botticelli · c. 1480 · Florence
Venus in a grove of citrus. The three Graces, Mercury, and the story of Chloris turning into Flora — a Medici wedding gift, decoded differently in every generation.
Europe
Pietà
High Renaissance · Michelangelo · 1498–1499 · Vatican
Carved by a 24-year-old. Mary is impossibly young — younger than her son — because, Michelangelo said, virtue preserves her. His only signed work.
Europe
A Sunday on La Grande Jatte
Post-Impressionism · Seurat · 1884–1886
Pointillism's founding canvas — ten feet wide, built entirely from tiny dots. Parisians on a Sunday afternoon, rendered like nothing before.
Europe
Water Lilies
Impressionism · Monet · 1896–1926 · Giverny
Two hundred and fifty paintings of the pond in his garden, late in life, eyes failing. The largest canvases wrap around the room at the Orangerie.
Europe
The Thinker
Modern · Rodin · 1880–1904
Originally a small figure above The Gates of Hell — Dante, looking down at his Inferno. Enlarged and cast on its own, it became a universal emblem of thought.
Europe
Wanderer above the Sea of Fog
German Romanticism · 1818
A man stands on a rocky peak, his back to us, looking into swirling cloud. Romanticism in one image — the sublime, and the lone self against it.
Europe
The Third of May 1808
Spanish Romanticism · 1814
A Madrid citizen, arms flung wide, faces a French firing squad in lantern-light. Painted as an accusation — art reporting on atrocity for the first time.
Europe
The Hay Wain
English Romanticism · 1821
A farm cart in a shallow stream on a summer afternoon. Nothing is happening. Constable turned rural quiet into monumental painting.
Europe
Le Déjeuner sur l'herbe
Early Modern · 1863
A nude woman picnics with two fully dressed men. The scandal wasn't the nudity — it was that she looks directly at the viewer, unashamed.
Europe
Olympia
Early Modern · 1863
A courtesan reclining nude, a Black maid bringing flowers, a cat raising its tail. The Salon hung it high to keep people from slashing it.
Europe
Melencolia I
Northern Renaissance · 1514
A winged figure sits surrounded by tools — compass, scales, hourglass — unable to act. Dürer's engraving of creative despair, loaded with symbols art historians still argue about.
Europe
The Calling of Saint Matthew
Baroque · 1599–1600 · Rome
Christ points across a shadowed tavern. A shaft of light slides down the back wall. Matthew gestures at himself — "me?" — caravaggio inventing a whole new visual language.
Europe
The Anatomy Lesson of Dr. Nicolaes Tulp
Dutch Golden Age · 1632
A public dissection of an executed criminal, witnessed by seven guild surgeons. Rembrandt was 26; it made his reputation.
Europe
The Milkmaid
Dutch Golden Age · c. 1660
A kitchen maid pours milk into a bowl. Morning light from the window. The whole painting is about the thin stream of milk and the pause it creates.
Europe
Hunters in the Snow
Northern Renaissance · 1565
Three hunters returning empty-handed to a frozen village; dogs slink behind them; skaters on the far ponds. One of the most lived-in winter scenes ever painted.
Europe
Liberty Leading the People
French Romanticism · 1830 · Louvre, Paris
Painted after the July Revolution that overthrew Charles X. Liberty strides over the barricades, bare-breasted, bayonet and tricolor raised. The boy with pistols later became Hugo's Gavroche.
Europe
The Raft of the Medusa
French Romanticism · 1818–1819 · Louvre, Paris
A real recent disaster: 147 men cut loose on a raft off Senegal after the captain took the lifeboats. Fifteen survived. Géricault visited morgues and built a model in his studio.
Europe
The Coronation of Napoleon
French Neoclassicism · 1805–1807 · Louvre, Paris
Nearly ten meters wide. Napoleon, having just placed the crown on his own head, is about to crown Joséphine. David inserted Napoleon's mother in the grandstand though she refused to attend.
Europe
The Wedding at Cana
Venetian Renaissance · 1563 · Louvre, Paris
The largest painting in the Louvre at seventy square meters. Painted for a Venetian refectory; Napoleon's troops cut it in half in 1797 to ship it to France.
Europe
The Lacemaker
Dutch Golden Age · c. 1669–1671 · Louvre, Paris
Only ten inches tall. A girl bent over her bobbins in concentrated silence — Vermeer's smallest canvas and one of his most intense studies of attention.
Europe
Psyche Revived by Cupid's Kiss
Neoclassical · 1793 · Louvre, Paris
Canova's marble of the moment Cupid wakes Psyche with a kiss. Designed to be circled — every angle offers a different composition.
Europe
La Grande Odalisque
French Neoclassicism · 1814 · Louvre, Paris
A concubine reclines on impossibly long, boneless anatomy. Critics counted extra vertebrae; Ingres was not interested in bones — he was interested in line.
Europe
Scrovegni Chapel Frescoes
Proto-Renaissance · 1303–1305 · Padua
Giotto's cycle of the life of the Virgin and Christ. A man paid for it to save his father from hell — his father appears in the painting, handing over the chapel model.
Europe
The Fighting Temeraire
English Romanticism · 1839
A storied warship, veteran of Trafalgar, tugged by a smudgy little steam tug to the breaker's yard. Turner's elegy for the age of sail — voted Britain's favorite painting.
Europe
Judith Beheading Holofernes
Baroque · c. 1599
Judith, arms extended to keep the blood off her dress, saws through the Assyrian general's neck. Caravaggio's first great biblical violence — the beginning of his reputation.
Europe
Sunflowers
Post-Impressionism · 1888
Painted in Arles to decorate Gauguin's guest bedroom. Van Gogh made seven versions; in letters, he called them an ode to gratitude.
Europe
View of Delft
Dutch Golden Age · c. 1660–1661
Vermeer's hometown across the water, clouds moving overhead. Proust called it "the most beautiful painting in the world" and had one of his characters die looking at it.
Europe
The Treachery of Images
Surrealism · 1929
A pipe painted with the words "Ceci n'est pas une pipe" (This is not a pipe). It isn't — it's a painting of one. Magritte's founding joke of Surrealism.
Europe
Mona Lisa (La Joconde)
c. 1503–1519 · Oil on poplar · Salle des États
Probably a portrait of Lisa Gherardini, wife of a Florentine merchant. Leonardo carried it with him for years, reworking it endlessly, and it never left his side until his death in France. Her fame owes as much to theft as to genius: when Vincenzo Peruggia stole her in 1911 and kept her in a Paris apartment for two years, the empty wall drew larger crowds than the painting ever had.
Europe
The Cheat with the Ace of Diamonds
c. 1636–1638 · Oil on canvas · Sully Wing
Four figures, four glances, one con. A naïve young nobleman is about to lose his fortune to the cheat on the left, who has pulled an ace from his belt. The courtesan and her servant are in on it; only the young man isn't looking at anyone.
Europe
The Dying Slave
c. 1513–1515 · Marble · Denon Wing
Intended for the tomb of Pope Julius II, a project that consumed Michelangelo for forty years and was never finished as planned. The figure is not dying but — perhaps — falling asleep, or surrendering to ecstasy. Michelangelo left the back deliberately rough.
Europe
The Marly Horses
1739–1745 · Marble · Cour Marly
Commissioned for the royal estate of Marly, where Louis XV wanted rearing horses at the entrance to his watering pond. Each is over 3.5 meters tall. They stood outdoors for nearly two centuries before being brought inside in 1984.
Europe
Crown of Louis XV
1722 · Silver gilt, originally set with the crown jewels · Galerie d'Apollon
Made for the coronation of the twelve-year-old Louis XV at Reims. The original diamonds — including the Régent, now displayed nearby — were removed after the ceremony and replaced with paste copies, which are what you see today.
Europe
The Napoleon III Apartments
1861 · Richelieu Wing
Not a single object but an entire suite of state rooms — crimson silk, gilded stucco, chandeliers heavy enough to frighten the ceiling. These were the ceremonial apartments of the Minister of State under the Second Empire. Walk in and the museum disappears.
Europe
The Régent Diamond
140.64 carats · Galerie d'Apollon
Found in India around 1698, smuggled by a slave who hid it in a wound in his leg, sold to Thomas Pitt (grandfather of William Pitt the Elder), and eventually bought by the French crown. Worn by Louis XV, Louis XVI, and set into Napoleon's sword. Still considered the most perfectly cut historical diamond in the world.
Europe
Drawings by Leonardo da Vinci
c. 1480–1510 · Chalk, ink, and silverpoint on paper
The Louvre holds over twenty sheets by Leonardo — studies of hands, horses, the tilt of a woman's head, the flow of water. A single silverpoint line, set down and never corrected.
Europe
Knight, Death and the Devil
1513 · Engraving
One of Dürer's three "Master Engravings." A Christian knight rides impassively through a desolate gorge, unmoved by the hourglass-bearing Death beside him or the goat-headed Devil behind. The technical summit of Renaissance printmaking.
Europe
Self-Portraits by Rembrandt
17th century · Etching
Rembrandt made over forty etched self-portraits across his lifetime. The Louvre holds a remarkable set. In ink on paper he is looser and more playful than in his painted selves — pulling faces, squinting, trying on hats.
Islamic World
Dome of the Rock
Umayyad · 691 CE · Jerusalem
The earliest surviving major Islamic building. The gold dome marks the rock from which Muhammad is said to have ascended on the Night Journey.
Islamic World
Mezquita of Córdoba
Umayyad of al-Andalus · 784 CE onward
A forest of red-and-white striped arches from a great caliphate. A cathedral was dropped into the middle of it in the 16th century.
Islamic World
Hagia Sophia
Byzantine & Ottoman · 537 CE onward · Istanbul
A Byzantine cathedral that held the largest dome on earth for a thousand years, then an Ottoman mosque, then a museum, now a mosque again.
Islamic World
Alhambra
Nasrid dynasty · 13th–14th c. · Granada, Spain
A palace-citadel of courtyards, fountains, and muqarnas ceilings. The poems inscribed on the walls are part of the architecture.
Islamic World
Registan of Samarkand
Timurid · 15th–17th c. · Uzbekistan
Three madrasas framing a public square — Ulugh Beg's was home to one of the greatest astronomical observatories of the medieval world.
Islamic World
Topkapı Palace
Ottoman · 15th–19th c. · Istanbul
The sultans' residence for 400 years. Unlike European palaces, it's a scatter of pavilions in a walled garden — closer to a fortified village.
Islamic World
Süleymaniye Mosque
Ottoman · Sinan · 1550–1557 · Istanbul
Mimar Sinan's masterpiece from the middle of his career — the architect's own tomb is in the garden outside.
Islamic World
Blue Mosque (Sultan Ahmed)
Ottoman · 1609–1616 · Istanbul
The Ottoman answer to Hagia Sophia across the square. More than 20,000 İznik tiles line the interior — the blue that gave it its Western name.
Islamic World
Shah Mosque, Isfahan
Safavid Persia · 1611–1629 · Iran
Seven-colour tilework on the scale of a whole square. Inside the dome, one clap echoes seven times.
Islamic World
Ardabil Carpet
Safavid Persia · 1539–1540 · Iran
Medallion carpet with 25 million knots. Made as a pair — the other half sold off piece by piece to repair this one. Now in the V&A.
Islamic World
İznik Pottery
Ottoman · 15th–17th c. · Anatolia
The workshop town that supplied tiles for mosques from Istanbul to Damascus. The signature tomato-red slip was a short-lived chemical triumph.
Islamic World
Great Mosque of Kairouan
Aghlabid · 836 · Tunisia
The oldest purpose-built mosque still standing. Its square minaret became the model for North African and Andalusi mosques for a thousand years.
Islamic World
Umayyad Mosque of Damascus
Umayyad · 715 · Syria
Built on a site already sacred to Jupiter, John the Baptist, and Mary. Byzantine mosaicists were brought in to lay the gold-ground interior.
Islamic World
Qutb Minar
Delhi Sultanate · 1192–1220 · Delhi
The tallest brick minaret in the world — 238 feet of red sandstone, carved with Qur'anic calligraphy as deeply as architecture.
Islamic World
Mosque of Ibn Tulun
Tulunid · 879 · Cairo
The oldest mosque in Cairo still in its original form. A vast brick courtyard under a spiral minaret — a shape borrowed from Samarra.
Islamic World
Po-i-Kalyan
Timurid · 12th–16th c. · Bukhara
A minaret, mosque, and madrasa across one square. Tradition says Genghis Khan spared the minaret because his hat fell off as he looked up at it.
Islamic World
Al-Azhar Mosque
Fatimid · 972 · Cairo
The mosque and university that became the intellectual center of the Sunni world. Continuously teaching since the tenth century.
Islamic World
Kutubiyya Mosque
Almohad · 1150s · Marrakech
A red sandstone minaret 250 feet tall — the template for the Giralda of Seville and the Hassan Tower of Rabat. Named for the booksellers who clustered at its base.
Islamic World
Jameh Mosque of Isfahan
Seljuq onward · 8th c. – modern · Iran
A thousand years of Iranian architecture layered in one building — two of the greatest domes in Islamic history stand a few meters apart.
Islamic World
Nasir al-Mulk Mosque
Qajar · 1888 · Shiraz
The "Pink Mosque" — stained-glass windows scatter colored light across Persian carpets every morning. Built late in the Islamic architectural tradition, as a kind of summary.
Islamic World
Gur-e Amir
Timurid · 1403 · Samarkand
The tomb of Timur under a ribbed turquoise dome. The grave was opened in 1941 with a curse; Germany invaded the USSR three days later.
Islamic World
Great Mosque of Samarra
Abbasid · 851 · Iraq
Once the largest mosque in the world. Its Malwiya minaret spirals upward 170 feet — a ramp you climb on the outside.
Islamic World
Mosque-Madrasa of Sultan Hasan
Mamluk · 1356–1363 · Cairo
A monumental stone box with a towering cruciform courtyard. One of the most audacious pieces of medieval Islamic architecture, by an unknown hand.
Islamic World
Shah-i-Zinda
Timurid · 11th–19th c. · Samarkand
A necropolis of tiled mausoleums along a single lane. The tomb complex for the cousin of the Prophet — and for every Timurid noble who wanted to lie near him.
Islamic World
Itchan Kala (Khiva)
Khanate of Khiva · 10th c. onward · Uzbekistan
The old inner town of Khiva — mud walls enclosing minarets, mausoleums, and palaces so untouched it can still look like a stage set.
Islamic World
Selimiye Mosque
Ottoman · Sinan · 1575 · Edirne
Sinan called it the masterpiece of his old age. Eight piers lift a dome wider than Hagia Sophia's — the height of Ottoman engineering.
Islamic World
Alcázar of Seville
Almohad → Mudéjar → Christian · 10th–14th c.
A royal palace begun by the Abbadids, expanded by Christian kings who kept the Islamic craftsmen — the result is Mudéjar: Moorish form under Castilian rule.
Islamic World
Giralda
Almohad minaret → Christian bell tower · 1184–1198
The Almohads built it as a minaret; after the Reconquista it became a cathedral bell tower. A ramp inside let the muezzin ride up on horseback.
Islamic World
Medina Azahara
Caliphate of Córdoba · 936–976 · Spain
The palace-city built outside Córdoba for Abd al-Rahman III — marble floors, mercury pools, gardens that moved. Destroyed in civil war within a century of completion.
Islamic World
Dome of Soltaniyeh
Ilkhanid Mongol · 1302–1312 · Iran
Second only to Hagia Sophia's in size when built. A blue-tiled double-shell dome soaring over the tomb of the Ilkhanid ruler Oljeitu — centuries ahead of its engineering.
Islamic World
Maqamat al-Hariri
Abbasid · al-Wasiti · 1237 · Baghdad
An illustrated manuscript of Hariri's picaresque stories — streets, markets, pilgrims, drunks, courts of law. The earliest surviving paintings of daily Baghdad life.
Islamic World
Shahnameh of Shah Tahmasp
Safavid · 16th c. · Iran
A royal copy of Ferdowsi's epic with 258 miniatures painted by the court workshop — mountains with gold streams, demons in the rocks. One of the great illuminated manuscripts.
Islamic World
Bibi-Khanym Mosque
Timurid · 1399–1404 · Samarkand
Timur commissioned it after sacking Delhi — the largest mosque in the Islamic world at the time. The main dome collapsed within decades; centuries later the Soviets rebuilt it.
Islamic World
Great Mosque of Herat
Ghurid onward · 12th c. · Afghanistan
The Friday Mosque of Herat, its courtyard walls tiled in a dense cobalt and turquoise mosaic. Its tile workshops still operate beside the mosque, rebuilding what time erodes.
Islamic World
Khamsa of Nizami
Persian · 12th c. poem, illuminated 14th c. onward
Five epic romances — Khosrow and Shirin, Layla and Majnun, and others — illustrated by every Persian royal workshop for centuries. The British Library copy alone has 30 miniatures.
Islamic World
Pyxis of al-Mughira
968 CE · Carved ivory · Cour Visconti
A small ivory box made in Córdoba for a teenage Umayyad prince. Every surface is carved with figures — musicians, lions, lovers, a man stealing eggs from an eagle's nest. The inscriptions include barely-veiled warnings about the instability of royal succession. Al-Mughira was executed by strangulation eight years later.
Islamic World
Baptistery of Saint Louis
c. 1320–1340 · Hammered brass inlaid with silver and gold · Cour Visconti
A Mamluk basin made in Egypt or Syria, covered in inlaid scenes of hunters, courtiers, and animals. At some point it entered the French royal treasury, and by tradition — probably invented — it was used for the baptism of royal children, including the future Louis XIII.
South Asia
Mohenjo-daro
Indus Valley Civilization · c. 2500 BCE · Pakistan
A Bronze Age city with grid streets, covered drains, and a Great Bath. Its script is still undeciphered.
South Asia
Great Stupa at Sanchi
Mauryan → Shunga · 3rd c. BCE onward
A hemispherical mound containing relics of the Buddha. The four carved gateways around it narrate his life without ever depicting him directly.
South Asia
Gandhara Buddha
Greco-Buddhist · 1st–5th c. CE · Pakistan/Afghanistan
The first sculptural images of the Buddha, made in schist and draped in togas. A crossroads style born from Alexander's successors.
South Asia
Ajanta Caves
Buddhist · 2nd c. BCE – 6th c. CE · Maharashtra
Thirty rock-cut monasteries with surviving murals of the Buddha's past lives. Abandoned, forgotten, and rediscovered by a tiger-hunting British officer in 1819.
South Asia
Nataraja (Shiva as Lord of Dance)
Chola dynasty · 10th–11th c. · Tamil Nadu
Shiva dances the universe into being and out of it again, surrounded by a ring of flame, trampling the demon of ignorance.
South Asia
Brihadeeswarar Temple
Chola dynasty · 1010 · Thanjavur
A granite tower 216 feet high, capped with a single 80-ton stone. A thousand years old and still in daily ritual use.
South Asia
Khajuraho Temples
Chandela dynasty · 950–1050 · Madhya Pradesh
Hindu and Jain temples covered in dense sculpture — gods, battles, and the frank erotic scenes the site is best known for.
South Asia
Konark Sun Temple
Eastern Ganga dynasty · 13th c. · Odisha
A massive stone chariot for the sun god, pulled by seven horses, wheels carved to tell the time from their shadows.
South Asia
Hampi (Vijayanagara)
Vijayanagara Empire · 14th–16th c. · Karnataka
Capital of the last great Hindu empire in the south. Sacked in 1565; the boulder-strewn ruins sprawl over 16 square miles.
South Asia
Meenakshi Temple
Nayak period · 16th–17th c. · Madurai
Fourteen gateway towers covered in thousands of painted figures. The whole complex is repainted every twelve years.
South Asia
Taj Mahal
Mughal · 1632–1653 · Agra
A tomb Shah Jahan built for his wife Mumtaz Mahal, who died in childbirth. The marble is inlaid with 28 kinds of gem and stone.
South Asia
Sigiriya
Anuradhapura period · 477 CE · Sri Lanka
A palace on top of a 660-foot granite plug. Its western face once carried 500 paintings of celestial women; twenty-one still survive.
South Asia
Bhimbetka Rock Shelters
Mesolithic onward · 30,000 BCE – historical · India
Painted caves with some of the oldest hunting scenes on earth — a human riding a gigantic red animal, drawn in ochre and charcoal.
South Asia
Fatehpur Sikri
Mughal · Akbar · 1571 · Uttar Pradesh
A red sandstone capital built and abandoned in fifteen years when the water ran out. The ghost city of the greatest of the Mughals.
South Asia
Dilwara Temples
Jain · 11th–13th c. · Mount Abu
White marble carved so finely the columns look more like lace than stone. Every surface is covered — floor, ceiling, arch.
South Asia
Elephanta Caves
Kalachuri / Chalukya · 5th–8th c. · Mumbai
Rock-cut Shiva temple on an island. The Trimurti — Shiva as creator, preserver, and destroyer — emerges twenty feet tall from the living stone.
South Asia
Shore Temple of Mahabalipuram
Pallava · c. 725 CE · Tamil Nadu
One of the earliest stone temples in South India. It stands on the Bay of Bengal; the 2004 tsunami briefly receded far enough to reveal more submerged temples.
South Asia
Pattadakal
Chalukya dynasty · 7th–8th c. · Karnataka
A proving ground for Indian temple architecture — northern and southern styles built side by side for direct comparison before being exported across the subcontinent.
South Asia
Ranakpur Jain Temple
Jain · 15th c. · Rajasthan
1,444 carved marble columns, no two alike. The complex is aligned so that the sun reaches the central sanctum for only a few days a year.
South Asia
Humayun's Tomb
Mughal · 1570 · Delhi
The first garden tomb of the Mughals and the prototype for the Taj Mahal eighty years later. A red sandstone dome in a four-quartered paradise garden.
South Asia
Golden Temple
Sikh · 16th–19th c. · Amritsar
The holiest site of Sikhism. Marble walls inlaid with gemstone, upper floors covered in gold leaf, the whole thing floating on a sacred pool.
South Asia
Hawa Mahal
Rajput · 1799 · Jaipur
The "Palace of Winds" — a honeycomb facade of 953 latticed windows, built so the women of the court could watch the street without being seen.
South Asia
Amber Fort
Rajput · 16th–18th c. · Jaipur
Hilltop fort-palace of the Kachhwaha dynasty. The Hall of Mirrors inside glitters with thousands of convex glass inlays; a single candle lights the whole room.
South Asia
Jantar Mantar
Rajput · Jai Singh II · 1734 · Jaipur
An observatory of huge masonry instruments — sundials ninety feet tall, instruments to locate any star. Built to correct the drifting calendars of Mughal India.
South Asia
Victoria Memorial
British Raj · 1906–1921 · Kolkata
A white marble domed memorial to Queen Victoria, built at the height of imperial self-regard. Now a museum of the Raj and the nationalism that ended it.
South Asia
Mughal Miniature Painting
Mughal · 16th–18th c. · North India
Album leaves commissioned by the Mughal emperors — historical chronicles, portraits, and natural history drawn with jeweller's precision.
South Asia
Lion Capital of Ashoka
Mauryan · c. 250 BCE · Sarnath
Four back-to-back lions atop a polished sandstone column, once topped by a wheel. Adopted as the national emblem of India in 1950.
South Asia
Gal Vihara
Polonnaruwa · 12th c. · Sri Lanka
Four colossal Buddhas carved from a single granite wall — seated, standing, reclining, meditating. The face of the reclining Buddha is nine meters long.
South Asia
Gol Gumbaz
Adil Shahi · 1656 · Bijapur
Tomb of Mohammed Adil Shah. The dome is the second-largest in the world, and the whispering gallery underneath it repeats a voice seven times.
South Asia
Red Fort
Mughal · Shah Jahan · 1648 · Delhi
Red sandstone walls two kilometers around. Inside, the Diwan-i-Khas held the Peacock Throne until Nadir Shah of Persia took it to Isfahan in 1739.
South Asia
Jama Masjid, Delhi
Mughal · Shah Jahan · 1656 · Delhi
The largest mosque in India — a red sandstone and marble courtyard for 25,000. Built in the same decade as the Taj Mahal, by the same emperor.
South Asia
Pancha Rathas
Pallava · 7th c. · Mahabalipuram
Five monolithic shrines carved from a single granite ridge in the shape of temple chariots. Each is a different architectural experiment — the prototypes for a millennium of South Indian temples.
South Asia
Nalanda Mahavihara
Pala dynasty · 5th–12th c. · Bihar
A Buddhist university that drew ten thousand monks from across Asia. Burned by Bakhtiyar Khalji around 1193; the library, they say, smoked for months.
South Asia
Mahabodhi Temple
Gupta → restored 19th c. · Bodh Gaya
Built at the spot the Buddha attained enlightenment under a Bodhi tree. A direct descendant of that tree still grows beside the temple.
South Asia
Akbar's Tomb
Mughal · 1605–1613 · Sikandra, Agra
A five-story mausoleum on a platform in a paradise garden. Akbar designed much of it himself; Jahangir's later changes produced the strange tiered result.
East Asia
Terracotta Army
Qin dynasty · 210 BCE · Xi'an, China
Over 8,000 soldiers, each with a different face, buried with the first emperor of China to guard him after death. Farmers digging a well found them in 1974.
East Asia
Great Wall of China
Ming (current form) · 14th–17th c.
Not one wall but many, rebuilt across dynasties. The Ming sections visitors see today are a fraction of a system that once stretched over 13,000 miles.
East Asia
Mogao Caves (Dunhuang)
Silk Road Buddhist · 4th–14th c. · Gansu
492 caves carved into cliff face, filled with murals and gilded clay Buddhas. One sealed chamber held 50,000 manuscripts — the largest trove of its kind.
East Asia
Hōryū-ji
Asuka period · c. 607 CE · Nara, Japan
Among the oldest wooden buildings still standing anywhere in the world. Built when Buddhism had only recently reached Japan.
East Asia
Goryeo Celadon
Goryeo dynasty · 10th–14th c. · Korea
Stoneware with a jade-green glaze so admired that Song-dynasty Chinese called Korean celadon the "first under heaven."
East Asia
Along the River During Qingming
Northern Song · Zhang Zeduan · 12th c.
A five-meter handscroll showing hundreds of people in the Song capital on a spring festival day. Read right to left — it unfolds as you walk past.
East Asia
Forbidden City
Ming & Qing · 1406–1420 onward · Beijing
980 buildings on 180 acres. For five centuries, commoners who entered uninvited were executed. It is now the world's most-visited museum.
East Asia
Kinkaku-ji (Golden Pavilion)
Muromachi · 1397, rebuilt 1955 · Kyoto
A retirement villa turned Zen temple, its top two floors wrapped in gold leaf. Burned down by a young monk in 1950 — rebuilt in facsimile.
East Asia
Ryōan-ji Rock Garden
Muromachi · late 15th c. · Kyoto
Fifteen stones in raked gravel. From no single vantage can you see all fifteen at once — you're always one short of a complete view.
East Asia
Himeji Castle
Edo period · 1609 · Hyōgo, Japan
The finest surviving Japanese castle, nicknamed "White Heron" for its white plaster. Its winding approach was designed to confuse besiegers.
East Asia
The Great Wave off Kanagawa
Edo Japan · Hokusai · c. 1831
A woodblock print sold for the price of a bowl of noodles. Mount Fuji sits small and steady under the claw of the wave. Hokusai was in his seventies.
East Asia
Longmen Grottoes
Northern Wei → Tang · 493 CE onward · Luoyang
Over 100,000 Buddhas carved into limestone cliffs along the Yi River. The largest is fifty-six feet tall and smiles.
East Asia
Yungang Grottoes
Northern Wei · 460–525 CE · Datong
Fifty-three caves with 51,000 statues. The earliest cliff Buddhas in China — faces still showing Indian and Central Asian lineage.
East Asia
Dazu Rock Carvings
Tang to Song · 9th–13th c. · Sichuan
Buddhist, Daoist, and Confucian figures carved side by side — a rare moment of syncretism preserved in stone.
East Asia
Crown of Silla
Silla kingdom · 5th–6th c. · Korea
Thin gold sheet cut into tree and antler forms, hung with comma-shaped jades and gold spangles. Too fragile to wear — meant for ritual and burial.
East Asia
Early Spring
Northern Song · Guo Xi · 1072 · China
A mountain landscape in ink and subtle color. Guo Xi said a painter should look at nature "as if going on a journey" — this scroll is that walk.
East Asia
Potala Palace
Tibetan · 1645 onward · Lhasa
Thirteen stories of winter residence for the Dalai Lamas — a thousand rooms, ten thousand shrines, two hundred thousand statues. Built where a 7th-century fort once stood.
East Asia
Great Buddha of Kamakura
Kamakura period · 1252 · Japan
A bronze Amida Buddha originally housed in a great hall — but tsunamis washed the building away in 1498 and left the statue to sit in the open ever since.
East Asia
Tōdai-ji
Nara period · 752 · Japan
For a thousand years the largest wooden building in the world. Inside, a 50-foot bronze Buddha, cast in eight pieces, seated in a lotus.
East Asia
Byōdō-in
Late Heian · 1053 · Uji
The Phoenix Hall — bird-shaped pavilion reflecting in its lotus pond. Built as a noble's villa; converted to a temple when he took the tonsure.
East Asia
Sanjūsangen-dō
Kamakura period · 1266 · Kyoto
A 390-foot hall holding 1,001 gilded statues of the thousand-armed Kannon. Each face is different; pilgrims look for their own.
East Asia
Nikkō Tōshō-gū
Early Edo · 1617 · Japan
The mausoleum of Tokugawa Ieyasu, built with the lavish polychrome carving the Tokugawa banned elsewhere. The three wise monkeys are here, above a stable.
East Asia
Summer Palace
Qing dynasty · 1750 onward · Beijing
Three square kilometres of gardens and pavilions around Kunming Lake. Sacked by Anglo-French forces in 1860, looted again by Boxer-era troops in 1900.
East Asia
Chinese Ritual Bronzes
Shang–Zhou · 16th–3rd c. BCE
Tripod cauldrons, wine vessels, and axes cast for ancestor worship. Each is wrapped in a monster mask (taotie) staring from every surface.
East Asia
Blue and White Porcelain
Yuan → Qing · 14th–19th c. · Jingdezhen
Cobalt under a clear glaze, fired to translucence. China's most-exported art form — copied in Iran, Turkey, Netherlands, and eventually the world.
East Asia
Prints of Kitagawa Utamaro
Edo Japan · 1790s
Utamaro specialized in the bijin-ga — studies of beautiful women — often just head and shoulders, in close-up. Scandalously intimate for the time.
East Asia
Netsuke
Edo Japan · 17th–19th c.
Miniature ivory or wood toggles that held purses to kimono sashes. Frogs, rats, mythic beasts — detailed enough to demand a magnifying glass.
East Asia
Admonitions Scroll
Six Dynasties · Gu Kaizhi (copy) · c. 380 CE
A handscroll of court ladies illustrating a Confucian poem of proper conduct. Probably the oldest surviving Chinese narrative painting, preserved in a Tang-era copy.
East Asia
Sanxingdui Bronze Masks
Sanxingdui culture · c. 1200 BCE · Sichuan
Hollow bronze and gold masks with protruding eyes and ears that fan outward. An entirely unknown civilization contemporary with Shang, found in two sacrificial pits in 1986.
East Asia
Temple of Heaven
Ming · 1420 · Beijing
A round blue-roofed temple on a square base — heaven over earth. Every winter solstice for five centuries, the emperor prayed here for the harvest.
East Asia
Kōfuku-ji
Nara period onward · 710 · Japan
One of Nara's four great monasteries. Its treasure hall holds the Asura statue — a three-faced, six-armed youth with an achingly thoughtful face.
East Asia
Seokguram Grotto
Unified Silla · 774 · Korea
A domed granite chamber holding a single seated Buddha, two and a half meters tall. The walls are carved with bodhisattvas and disciples — a sculptural mandala.
East Asia
Night-Shining White
Tang dynasty · Han Gan · c. 750 · China
A tethered stallion struggling at the post — snorting, hooves kicking. The emperor Xuanzong's favorite horse, painted in ink so alive it almost leaves the silk.
East Asia
Red and White Plum Blossoms
Edo Japan · Ogata Kōrin · early 18th c.
A pair of folding screens: two plum trees, red and white, lean toward a stylized silver river of whorls. Rinpa-school decorative painting at its most distilled.
East Asia
Fine Wind, Clear Morning (Red Fuji)
Edo Japan · Hokusai · c. 1830–1832
Hokusai's other famous Fuji — a single triangular form lit crimson by morning sun, clouds streaming off the summit. From the same series as the Great Wave.
East Asia
Cypress Trees
Momoyama Japan · Kanō Eitoku · 1590
An eight-panel folding screen with a single enormous cypress sprawling across gold leaf. The Kano school's monumental court style at its peak.
East Asia
Actor Prints of Sharaku
Edo Japan · Tōshūsai Sharaku · 1794–1795
Kabuki actors caught mid-gesture, their exaggerated faces rendered in crisp black outline against mica backgrounds. Sharaku worked for ten months and then vanished.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Borobudur
Sailendra dynasty · 9th c. · Java, Indonesia
A stone mandala you walk. Climbing its nine terraces takes you through 2,672 relief panels and 504 Buddhas to the empty stupa at the top.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Prambanan
Medang Kingdom · 9th c. · Java, Indonesia
The Hindu counterpart to Borobudur a few miles away — a cluster of tall, sharp-edged temples to the Trimurti, walls carved with the Ramayana.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Angkor Wat
Khmer Empire · 12th c. · Cambodia
The largest religious monument on earth. Built as a Hindu temple to Vishnu, converted to Buddhist use, and never fully abandoned.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Bagan
Pagan Kingdom · 11th–13th c. · Myanmar
More than 2,000 Buddhist temples and stupas on a plain by the Irrawaddy. At dawn hot air balloons drift over a landscape of brick towers.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Wat Phra Kaew
Rattanakosin · 1782 onward · Bangkok
The royal temple inside the Grand Palace, home to the Emerald Buddha — carved from a single block of jade, robed differently for each season.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Nan Madol
Saudeleur dynasty · 12th–17th c. · Pohnpei
A city of 92 artificial islets built from hexagonal basalt logs, laid across a lagoon. The "Venice of the Pacific" — and a place no one has fully explained.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Moai
Rapa Nui · 1250–1500 · Easter Island
Nearly 900 stone figures, carved and moved miles from the quarry. They face inland — watching over the villages, not out to sea.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Asmat Carving
Asmat people · Papua, Indonesia
Bisj poles and shields carved from mangrove with sharpened bone. Each pole commemorates a death and demands it be avenged.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Māori Wharenui
Aotearoa · 18th c. onward · New Zealand
A carved meeting house represents an ancestor's body — ridgepole as spine, rafters as ribs. Every panel tells a story in whakairo carving.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Tā Moko
Māori · pre-European onward · New Zealand
Tattoos chiseled (not punctured) into the skin, carrying genealogy and rank. Each design is unique to the person who wears it.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Ubirr Rock Art
Aboriginal Australian · up to 20,000 years old · Kakadu
One of the oldest continuous art traditions on earth. Later paintings show fish anatomy so precisely that species are still identifiable.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Mỹ Sơn
Champa Kingdom · 4th–14th c. · Vietnam
Brick Hindu temples in a jungle valley. Seventy stood before American bombs in 1969; twenty survive, still slowly giving up their secrets.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Ayutthaya
Ayutthaya Kingdom · 14th–18th c. · Thailand
The Thai capital before the Burmese sacked it in 1767. Brick prangs lean at odd angles around the old moated island.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Sukhothai
Sukhothai Kingdom · 13th–15th c. · Thailand
Serene standing Buddhas in the "walking" pose invented here — one hand raised, the other swinging at the hip, fabric flowing forward.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Wayang Kulit
Java · 9th c. onward · Indonesia
Flat leather shadow puppets of the Ramayana and Mahabharata, manipulated against a backlit screen. A traditional performance runs all night.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Đông Sơn Culture
Bronze Age · 700 BCE – 100 CE · Vietnam
Bronze drums cast with spiral patterns and concentric bird-men. Traded across mainland Southeast Asia — a pre-Indic shared world.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Bayon
Khmer Empire · late 12th c. · Cambodia
The last great temple of Angkor — 200 serene faces carved into its towers, likely portraits of Jayavarman VII himself, watching from every direction.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Preah Vihear
Khmer Empire · 9th–11th c. · Cambodia
A cliff-top temple on the Thai border — 800 meters of causeway climbing to a sanctuary looking out over the plain. Thailand and Cambodia fought over its ownership in 2011.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Balinese Temples
Balinese Hindu · 9th c. onward · Bali
Every village has at least three: for the ancestors, for daily life, for the dead. Split gates, black palm-fiber roofs, statues wrapped in checkered cloth.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Indigenous Australian Art
Aboriginal · millennia to present · Australia
Bradshaw paintings (Gwion Gwion) in the Kimberley, possibly 20,000 years old — elongated figures painted with pigment that has bonded permanently with the rock surface.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Uluru
Aṉangu · sacred site · Australia
A sandstone monolith rising 1,100 feet out of the center of the continent. For the Aṉangu it is the physical record of ancestral beings' creation journeys.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Angkor Thom
Khmer Empire · late 12th c. · Cambodia
The last capital of the Khmer Empire — nine square kilometers walled in laterite. At its heart, the Bayon; at its gates, giants pulling serpents in cosmic tug-of-war.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Ananda Temple
Pagan Kingdom · 1105 · Myanmar
A cruciform white temple with four standing Buddhas, each eighteen feet tall. From a distance the gold umbrella on its stupa is the last thing to disappear.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Besakih Temple
Balinese Hindu · 14th c. onward · Bali
The Mother Temple of Bali — 23 linked sanctuaries up the slope of Mount Agung. When Agung erupted in 1963, lava stopped meters from its gates.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Banaue Rice Terraces
Ifugao · 2000+ years old · Philippines
Stone-walled terraces climbing the mountainside. Built without wheeled carts, draft animals, or cement. If unwrapped they'd circle half the globe.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Plain of Jars
Iron Age · 500 BCE – 500 CE · Laos
Thousands of carved stone jars scattered across a plateau. Probably funerary; ground-truth research has been slow because the plain is still littered with unexploded American bombs.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Shwedagon Pagoda
Mon → Burmese · 6th–11th c. onward · Yangon
A gilded stupa 325 feet tall, covered in gold leaf and crowned with 4,000 diamonds. Said to enshrine hairs of the Buddha.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Banteay Srei
Khmer Empire · 967 · Cambodia
"Citadel of Women" — a small temple of pink sandstone carved with almost impossibly fine reliefs. Malraux tried to steal four figures in 1923 and was arrested.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Tongkonan
Toraja · centuries old · Sulawesi, Indonesia
Ancestral houses with boat-shaped roofs soaring up at both ends. Each is built facing north, aligned to the cosmos of the Torajan dead.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Hei-tiki
Māori · pre-European onward · New Zealand
Nephrite pendants of a human figure worn around the neck, passed down through generations. Each is named; each gains mana from the people who wear it.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Rongorongo
Rapa Nui · pre-contact? · Easter Island
Glyphs carved onto wooden tablets — one of the few independent writing systems in human history. Still undeciphered; most tablets were burned by missionaries.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Candi Sukuh
Majapahit · 15th c. · Java
A Hindu temple built as a truncated stone pyramid — closer to Maya than to Angkor. Its reliefs are frank about fertility; the statues at the gate tell you what they're for.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Moon of Pejeng
Dong Son tradition · c. 300 BCE · Bali
The largest bronze kettledrum in the world — almost two meters across. In Balinese legend it's the fallen wheel of a chariot of the moon.
Southeast Asia & Oceania
Wat Rong Khun (White Temple)
Contemporary Thai Buddhist · Chalermchai Kositpipat · 1997 onward · Chiang Rai
An entire temple sculpted white, glittering with shards of mirror. The artist intends it to take centuries to complete; hands rise from hell at the entrance.
Americas
Olmec Colossal Heads
Olmec · 1200–400 BCE · Gulf coast, Mexico
Seventeen basalt heads, up to ten feet tall, each with a distinct face. Carved with stone tools and floated on rafts from the quarry, 60 miles away.
Americas
Teotihuacan
Pre-Aztec · 100 BCE – 550 CE · Mexico
A city of over 100,000 at its peak — larger than Rome at the same time. Already abandoned when the Aztecs found it and named it "the place where gods were born."
Americas
Tikal
Classic Maya · 400 BCE – 900 CE · Guatemala
Step pyramids rising above the rainforest canopy. A Maya superpower that swallowed rival cities until a long drought took it down.
Americas
Palenque
Classic Maya · 226 BCE – 799 CE · Chiapas
A small Maya kingdom with exquisite stucco portraits and one of the most carefully decoded royal tombs in the Americas — that of Pakal the Great.
Americas
Moche Culture
Moche · 100–700 CE · Northern Peru
Before the Inca, the Moche built painted pyramids and made portrait pots so individual you can tell one person from another across fifteen hundred years.
Americas
Nazca Lines
Nazca culture · 500 BCE – 500 CE · Peru
Hummingbirds, monkeys, and spiders drawn at the scale of football fields by clearing pebbles from the desert floor. Visible only from the air.
Americas
Chichen Itza
Post-Classic Maya · 600–1200 CE · Yucatán
On the spring equinox, sunlight strikes the pyramid's stairway to form a serpent that seems to slither down the temple of Kukulkan.
Americas
Coatlicue
Mexica · late 15th c. · Tenochtitlan
"She of the Serpent Skirt" — the earth goddess, severed neck bleeding two snakes, wearing a necklace of hands and hearts. Found under Mexico City in 1790.
Americas
Aztec Sun Stone
Mexica · late 15th c. · Tenochtitlan
A basalt disk over three meters across, carving the five successive suns of Aztec cosmology. Buried under the Zócalo in Mexico City until 1790.
Americas
Sacsayhuamán
Inca · 15th c. · Cusco, Peru
A fortress-temple above Cusco built from stones weighing up to 200 tons, fitted so precisely a sheet of paper won't pass between them.
Americas
Machu Picchu
Inca · 15th c. · Peru
A royal estate on a knife-edged ridge. The stones of its walls are cut so precisely that no mortar holds them — and they have survived five centuries of earthquakes.
Americas
Monte Albán
Zapotec · 500 BCE – 850 CE · Oaxaca
A leveled mountaintop city with one of the earliest writing systems in the Americas. Its danzantes reliefs show contorted, stripped captives.
Americas
Copán
Classic Maya · 400–820 CE · Honduras
A small Maya kingdom with the finest stone sculpture in the Americas. Stelae-portraits of kings carved nearly in the round.
Americas
Mesa Verde Cliff Palace
Ancestral Pueblo · 12th–13th c. · Colorado
Villages tucked under sandstone overhangs. The Ancestral Puebloans built, lived in, and abandoned them in a little over a century.
Americas
Tiwanaku
Tiwanaku · 500–1000 CE · Bolivia
A highland capital on the shore of Lake Titicaca. Its Gate of the Sun — cut from a single stone — faces the point where the sun rises at equinox.
Americas
Chavín de Huántar
Chavín · 900–200 BCE · Peru
A mountain temple with underground galleries and the Lanzón, a carved granite blade — Peru's oldest pan-Andean god, still in his original chamber.
Americas
Chan Chan
Chimú · 900–1470 CE · Peru
The largest adobe city in the Americas. Twelve miles of mud-brick walls carved with rows of seabirds, fish, and waves.
Americas
Atlantean Figures of Tula
Toltec · 10th–12th c. · Mexico
Fifteen-foot basalt warriors in feathered headdresses on top of Pyramid B. They held up the roof of a temple that is long gone.
Americas
Cueva de las Manos
Patagonian hunter-gatherers · 7300–700 BCE · Argentina
A cave with hundreds of human hand stencils, mostly left hands — because the artists were holding the bone tube of pigment in the right, blowing around it.
Americas
Cahokia Mounds
Mississippian · 1050–1350 · Illinois
The largest city in North America before Europeans arrived — bigger than London in its day. Monks Mound alone is a hundred feet tall, built of earth by hand.
Americas
Serpent Mound
Fort Ancient or Adena · c. 1070 CE · Ohio
A quarter-mile-long effigy mound of a snake swallowing an egg. The head aligns with sunset at the summer solstice; the coils seem to encode lunar cycles.
Americas
Northwest Coast Totem Poles
Haida, Tlingit, Kwakwaka'wakw · 18th–20th c.
Cedar poles carved with family lineage, clan animals, and narrated stories. Each is a library — readable top to bottom by those who know the figures.
Americas
Navajo Weaving
Diné · 17th c. onward · American Southwest
Tightly woven wool rugs and wearing blankets — the weaver puts a deliberate flaw into each one, a ch'į́įdii line, so the spirit of the piece has a way out.
Americas
Chaco Canyon
Ancestral Pueblo · 850–1250 · New Mexico
A ceremonial center of "great houses" — multi-story sandstone buildings in a treeless canyon. Roads radiate out straight for sixty miles, aligned to the sky.
Americas
Taos Pueblo
Tiwa · 1000+ years continuous · New Mexico
Adobe pueblos stacked five stories high. Continuously inhabited longer than any community in the U.S. — the family on the top floor climbs ladders up and down.
Americas
Quimbaya Goldwork
Quimbaya · 300–1550 CE · Colombia
Cast gold figures of caciques, poporos, and seated shamans. In 1892 a diplomat gave 122 of them to Spain — one of the largest pre-Columbian collections anywhere.
Americas
Lord of Sipán Tomb
Moche · c. 290 CE · Peru
The intact grave of a Moche warrior-priest, draped in gold, silver, turquoise, and Spondylus. Discovered in 1987 moments ahead of looters.
Americas
Spirit of Haida Gwaii
Haida · Bill Reid · 1986–1991
A bronze canoe filled with thirteen mythic figures paddling together — Raven, Bear, Eagle, Wolf, and others. Reid worked on it for five years while Parkinson's overtook him.
Americas
Maria Martinez Blackware
San Ildefonso Pueblo · 20th c. · New Mexico
Maria and her husband Julian revived and reinvented matte-on-black pueblo pottery. Their pots sit in every major American museum — and still on shelves at San Ildefonso.
Americas
Ghost Dance Shirt
Lakota · 1890 · Great Plains
Hide shirts painted with stars and eagles, worn in the Ghost Dance — believed to turn aside bullets. Shirts were collected from the dead at Wounded Knee.
Americas
Pyramid of the Magician
Late Classic Maya · 600–900 CE · Uxmal
An oval-based pyramid whose legend says it was built in one night by a dwarf magician. Every stone is carved; the central doorway is a monster's open mouth.
Americas
Tumi Ceremonial Knife
Lambayeque · 900–1100 · Northern Peru
A gold sacrificial knife topped with the long-eared god Naylamp, eyes inlaid with turquoise. Used for ritual decapitation; now a symbol of Peru on souvenir shelves everywhere.
Americas
Coyolxāuhqui Stone
Mexica · late 15th c. · Tenochtitlan
A twelve-foot disk of the moon goddess, dismembered by her brother Huitzilopochtli. Found by electric-company workers in Mexico City in 1978 — it launched modern excavation of the Templo Mayor.
Americas
Paquimé (Casas Grandes)
Mogollon · 13th–14th c. · Chihuahua, Mexico
Multi-story adobe pueblos north of the Maya world but connected to them — Paquimé traded in parrots, copper bells, and turquoise. Burned and abandoned before the Spanish arrived.
No works match that search.
Images via Wikimedia Commons. Click any image to open it and see its source.
